Based on available documentation: Section 8 includes five end-to-end integration scenarios, but the retrieved documentation only provides the detailed name and walkthrough for Scenario A. [chunk-kb-sample-document-with-image-1_doc-dce-integration-platform-implementation-plan_chunk-0044][chunk-kb-sample-document-with-image-1_doc-dce-integration-platform-implementation-plan_chunk-0043][chunk-kb-sample-document-with-image-1_doc-dce-integration-platform-implementation-plan_chunk-0045]

1. **Scenario A — Customer service request submission (async transaction):** A customer raises a service request through the portal, receives an immediate acknowledgement, and backend processing continues asynchronously. [chunk-kb-sample-document-with-image-1_doc-dce-integration-platform-implementation-plan_chunk-0044]  
2. When D365 confirms creation, the portal is updated in real time via an event push, without polling. [chunk-kb-sample-document-with-image-1_doc-dce-integration-platform-implementation-plan_chunk-0044]  

The five scenario patterns covered by Section 8 are synchronous queries, asynchronous transactions, saga-style multi-system orchestrations, inbound event-driven flows, and batch data movement. [chunk-kb-sample-document-with-image-1_doc-dce-integration-platform-implementation-plan_chunk-0044]

section_path: 8.1 Scenario A — Customer service request submission (async transaction)

retrieved_chunks.chunk_text:
8. Integration scenarios (end-to-end walkthroughs)

This section walks through five concrete end-to-end scenarios that exercise the main integration patterns supported by the platform. Each scenario covers a real customer portal use case and illustrates how the components interact in sequence. Together they demonstrate that the architecture handles synchronous queries, asynchronous transactions, saga-style multi-system orchestrations, inbound event-driven flows, and batch data movement — without requiring any custom platform code.

Sequence diagram conventions:

•

Solid arrow

— synchronous request / response

•

Dashed teal arrow

— asynchronous message (queue, Service Bus)

•

Dotted purple arrow

— event publication (Event Grid, CloudEvents)

•

Dashed grey arrow with open head

— response / return

•

Dot-dash amber arrow

— batch operation

3.1 Platform composition over platform construction

Use Microsoft-maintained PaaS services to compose the integration layer. Do not build platform capabilities (gateway, orchestration engine, connector framework, monitoring stack) that already exist as production Azure services. Custom code is only written for integration-specific business logic, not platform infrastructure.

3.2 Reference model by default, deviation by exception

All integrations follow Microsoft's reference architecture pattern (APIM front, Logic Apps / Service Bus / Event Grid behind) unless there is a documented, approved exception. Deviations require architecture review and explicit justification.

3.3 Pattern-fit per integration scenario

Not every integration is the same. Synchronous queries (e.g., inventory check at checkout), asynchronous transactions (e.g., order placement), event-driven push (e.g., D365 order status change), and batch synchronisation (e.g., Snowflake DW load) each require different patterns. No single pattern is imposed across the board; each scenario is matched to the right pattern.

7. Integration patterns by system

**System** | **Primary pattern** | **Logic Apps connector** | **Auth model** | **Notes**
----
D365 F&O | Sync + async + event | Native (first-party) | AAD OAuth (App reg) | Sync for queries; async via Service Bus for transactions; Event Grid for business events
D365 CE | Sync + event | Native (first-party) | AAD OAuth | Sync via Dataverse Web API; events via Event Grid + Dataverse webhooks
Snowflake | Batch + sync | Native | Key-pair (preferred) | Bulk ETL via Data Factory; ad-hoc queries via Logic Apps
Email service | Async fire-and-forget | Native (SendGrid / O365) | API key / OAuth | Template management externalised
Azure AD B2C | Sync (auth flow) | Native (Graph API) | AAD OAuth | Custom policies for B2C user journeys; Graph for admin operations
Ricoh MPS | Event ingest + sync | Custom HTTP | TBD (likely mTLS / API key) | Proprietary system; custom Logic Apps HTTP integration or IoT Hub for telemetry scale
ServiceNow | Sync + webhook | Native | OAuth | Cases and tickets via REST; status updates via inbound webhooks to Event Grid
Payment gateway | Sync + webhook | Native (if Stripe/Adyen) or Custom HTTP | Gateway-specific tokenisation | PCI-scope minimised by tokenisation; webhooks for async settlement updates
Docuware | Sync (upload + retrieve) | Custom HTTP | OAuth / API key | REST API integration; binary upload via multipart

7.2 D365 Customer Engagement (CRM)

D365 CE holds the customer master, service cases, opportunities, and other CRM data. Integration is primarily synchronous via the Dataverse Web API, complemented by event-driven push for case status changes and customer record updates.

•

Customer profile sync

(sync via Logic App D365 CE connector) on portal sign-up and profile update; create or update the matching Dataverse contact record.

•

Service request submission

(async via Service Bus) when a customer raises a request through the portal; case is created in D365 CE and routed.

•

Case status updates

(event push) so the portal reflects the current case status without polling.

7.3 Snowflake

Snowflake is the data warehouse, integrated primarily via batch ETL using Data Factory. The portal does not write to Snowflake directly; instead, all transactional data flows into Snowflake via scheduled pipelines from D365 and other sources.

•

Daily D365 → Snowflake load

(Data Factory pipeline) extracts incremental changes from D365 OData, stages in ADLS Gen2, transforms, and loads into Snowflake staging tables.

•

Snowflake → portal analytics

(Logic App or APIM-cached query) exposes aggregated metrics (e.g., monthly usage summaries) to the portal.

•

Authentication

via Snowflake key-pair (rotated quarterly), credentials stored in Key Vault.

1. Executive summary

This document specifies the implementation plan for the integration layer connecting the Spryker-based DCE customer portal to nine enterprise systems across two Azure tenants. The architecture is built on Azure native iPaaS services — API Management (APIM) as the gateway, Logic Apps and Functions for orchestration, Service Bus for asynchronous messaging, Event Grid for event-driven push, Data Factory for batch synchronisation, Application Insights for observability, and Azure AD B2C for customer identity.

The approach intentionally avoids building a custom middleware application. Instead, it leverages Microsoft-maintained PaaS services, which already provide the platform capabilities (pluggable connectors, multi-protocol support, identity federation, orchestration, monitoring) that a custom middleware would need to reproduce. This aligns to Ricoh's existing Azure enterprise standard and Microsoft's reference integration architecture, eliminates vendor platform lock-in, and provides a sustainable foundation for future integrations beyond the customer portal scope.

The plan is structured around a three-phase rollout: a Foundation phase (2–4 weeks) to establish landing-zone and governance; a Pilot phase (3 months, aligned to the Validation SOW) delivering the first end-to-end D365 F&O integration; and Expansion phases (6–12 months) onboarding the remaining systems following the same patterns. The estimated three-year total cost of ownership is materially lower than the custom middleware alternative, primarily because the integration platform itself does not require building or maintaining.

Critical success factors include: (a) securing Azure iPaaS expertise via a Microsoft Gold Partner or internal Azure capability, (b) investing in upfront architecture and governance design before building integrations, and (c) per-system fit validation of pre-built connectors against specific use cases. Risks are surfaced and mitigation strategies are proposed.
